title: Food chains and food webs — energy transfer
examTip: Energy transfer calculations are a favourite AQA target — know that only about 10% of energy passes from one trophic level to the next, and be able to calculate efficiency. Biomass pyramids are always drawn with producers at the base. Understand why energy is lost at each level and what implications that has for food chain length and farming efficiency.
introTips: Food chains show who eats who — but the real point is energy transfer. Most energy is LOST at each step.
AQA will give you energy values and ask you to calculate efficiency. Know the formula: (energy at next level ÷ energy at previous level) × 100.
Understand why it's more energy-efficient to eat plants than meat — this comes up in sustainability questions too.
intro: Every food chain begins with a producer — usually a plant — that captures energy from the sun through photosynthesis. That energy is passed from organism to organism through eating and being eaten. But at each step, most of the energy is lost — through heat, movement, and excretion. Understanding this energy loss explains why food chains are rarely longer than 4 or 5 links, and why eating lower in the food chain is more efficient.

keyPoints.heading: Food chains — producers, consumers and decomposers
keyPoints.content: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from one organism to the next. It always starts with a producer — an organism that makes its own food using energy from the sun (photosynthesis). Producers are usually green plants or algae. Each organism that eats another is a consumer. Primary consumers eat producers (herbivores). Secondary consumers eat primary consumers. Tertiary consumers eat secondary consumers. Each level is called a trophic level. Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down dead organisms and waste, returning minerals to the soil — they are essential for nutrient cycling.
writeBullets: Food chain always starts with a producer (plant/alga — uses photosynthesis)
Primary consumer eats producer (herbivore)
Secondary consumer eats primary consumer
Tertiary consumer eats secondary consumer
Each level = a trophic level
Decomposers: bacteria/fungi break down dead matter → return minerals to soil

keyTerms.def: An organism that makes its own food using light energy (photosynthesis) — the first trophic level in any food chain
An organism that obtains energy by eating other organisms
A feeding level in a food chain — producers are at trophic level 1, primary consumers at level 2, etc.
An organism (bacterium or fungus) that breaks down dead organic matter, releasing minerals back into the ecosystem

keyPoints.memTip: Food chain = energy flowing from the sun through producers → consumers. Decomposers don't fit in a chain — they break down everything at every level. Producer → Primary → Secondary → Tertiary = levels 1 → 2 → 3 → 4.

keyPoints.heading: Energy transfer and efficiency
keyPoints.content: When one organism eats another, not all the energy is transferred. A large proportion of energy at each trophic level is lost in several ways: energy used in respiration (movement, staying warm, metabolic processes); energy lost in faeces (indigestible material); energy lost as heat. Only about 10% of the energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next trophic level (stored in biomass). This limits food chain length — by the time you reach the fourth or fifth trophic level, so little energy remains that there isn't enough to support another level. Efficiency of energy transfer = (energy transferred to next level ÷ energy available at current level) × 100.
writeBullets: Only ~10% of energy passes from one trophic level to the next
Energy lost via: respiration (heat/movement), faeces, urine
Each transfer is roughly 90% efficient at LOSING energy
Energy transfer efficiency (%) = (energy at next level ÷ energy at this level) × 100
Low efficiency limits food chain length — usually max 4–5 trophic levels

keyTerms.def: The percentage of energy from one trophic level that is passed on to the next — typically around 10%
The total mass of living material in organisms at a given trophic level — decreases at each level up the food chain

keyPoints.memTip: 10% rule: each level passes on about 10% of its energy. So if producers have 10,000 J, primary consumers have ~1,000 J, secondary consumers ~100 J, tertiary ~10 J. By level 5 there's almost nothing left. That's why food chains stop at 4–5 links.

keyPoints.heading: Pyramids of biomass and energy
keyPoints.content: A pyramid of biomass shows the total mass of organisms at each trophic level. Producers are always at the base, with the largest biomass. Each successive level is smaller because biomass is lost at each transfer. Pyramids of biomass are almost always a true pyramid shape (unlike pyramids of numbers, which can be inverted — e.g. one tree supports many insects). A pyramid of energy shows energy available at each trophic level — these are always pyramid-shaped because energy is always lost at each transfer. AQA may ask you to draw or interpret these diagrams.
writeBullets: Pyramid of biomass: shows total mass at each trophic level — producers at base (most biomass)
Almost always true pyramid shape (narrowing at each level)
Pyramid of numbers: shows number of organisms — can be inverted (one tree, many aphids)
Pyramid of energy: always true pyramid — energy always decreases up the chain
Draw with producers at the bottom — never at the top

keyTerms.def: A diagram showing the total mass of organisms at each trophic level — producers at the base, always pyramid-shaped
A diagram showing the number of organisms at each trophic level — can be inverted if producers are very large organisms (e.g. a single tree)

keyPoints.memTip: Biomass pyramid = almost always true pyramid shape. Numbers pyramid can be inverted. Energy pyramid = always pyramid. If the question says 'draw a pyramid', always put producers at the BOTTOM. Never invert a biomass or energy pyramid.

keyPoints.heading: Food webs and the consequences of change
keyPoints.content: A food web shows multiple overlapping food chains in an ecosystem. Most organisms eat more than one thing and are eaten by more than one predator. This interconnection means that changes to one species cause knock-on effects throughout the web. If a prey species decreases: predators that eat it may decrease (less food), and species the prey used to eat may increase (less predation). AQA often gives you a food web and asks you to predict what happens if one species changes. Always trace the effects through every connected species — up and down the web.
writeBullets: Food web = multiple overlapping food chains showing real feeding relationships
Organisms usually eat several different prey and are eaten by multiple predators
If one species decreases: predators may decrease, prey may increase
If one species increases: may cause decrease in prey, increase in their predators
Must trace effects through all connected species — not just one step

keyTerms.def: A diagram showing the feeding relationships between many species in an ecosystem — a network of overlapping food chains

keyPoints.memTip: Food web questions = follow the arrows. Arrow direction = energy flow = who eats who. If rabbits disappear: foxes have less food (↓), grass/plants have less herbivores eating them (↑). Always check both directions — what was eating the rabbit, and what was the rabbit eating?

tables.title: Energy loss at each trophic level

0.question: Why is only about 10% of energy transferred from one trophic level to the next?
options: Organisms at higher trophic levels are larger and need less energy
Most energy is lost through respiration, movement, heat and faeces and is not stored in biomass
Producers do not make enough glucose for higher consumers to use
Energy is destroyed when organisms are eaten
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — energy is lost as heat through respiration, in movement, and in indigestible material in faeces. Only what's stored in biomass passes to the next level.
0.wrongFeedback: Energy is not destroyed — it's transferred to the surroundings as heat. Most energy at each trophic level is used in respiration (for movement, maintaining body temperature, all metabolic processes) or lost in faeces. Only the energy stored in new biomass (growth) is available to the next trophic level — approximately 10%.

2.question: A producer contains 20,000 kJ of energy. The primary consumers contain 1,800 kJ. What is the efficiency of energy transfer?
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — (1,800 ÷ 20,000) × 100 = 9%. Close to the typical ~10% figure.
2.wrongFeedback: Efficiency = (energy transferred ÷ energy available) × 100 = (1,800 ÷ 20,000) × 100 = 9%. Always divide the NEXT level's energy by the CURRENT level's energy — not the other way around.

commonMistakes: Saying 'energy is lost' without saying where it goes — energy transferred to the surroundings as heat (via respiration) and lost in faeces. Always state the mechanism.
Drawing pyramids with producers at the top — producers always go at the BASE. The smallest trophic level (tertiary consumers) goes at the top.
Forgetting decomposers in food chain descriptions — they break down dead matter at every level and return minerals to the soil for producers to use.
When tracing food web effects, only following one step — if rabbits decrease, foxes decrease (less food) AND grass increases (less herbivory). Always follow all connections.
Confusing biomass and energy pyramids with number pyramids — numbers can be inverted; biomass and energy pyramids are always true pyramids.
examTips: For efficiency calculations: efficiency (%) = (energy at next level ÷ energy at current level) × 100. If given biomass instead of energy, use the same formula with mass values.
For food web 'predict what happens' questions: identify what the changed species eats and what eats it, then trace the effect up AND down the web.
When explaining why energy is lost at each trophic level, give specific reasons: respiration (heat), movement, waste in faeces/urine — don't just say 'some energy is lost'.
Pyramid of biomass vs numbers: if asked which type an unusual shape could be, numbers is the answer (can be inverted). Biomass is almost always a true pyramid.
revisionCardBullets: Food chain starts with producer (photosynthesis) → primary → secondary → tertiary consumer
Decomposers (bacteria/fungi) break down dead matter → return minerals to producers
Only ~10% of energy transferred from one trophic level to the next
Energy lost via: respiration (heat), movement, faeces
Efficiency (%) = (energy at next level ÷ energy at current level) × 100
Low efficiency limits food chain length to ~4–5 trophic levels
Pyramid of biomass: producers at base — almost always true pyramid shape
Pyramid of numbers: can be inverted (e.g. one tree, many insects)
Pyramid of energy: always true pyramid — energy always decreases up the chain
Food web: if one species changes, trace knock-on effects through all connected species
